ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette food product class; no specific cheese-ham omelette subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers omelette as a generic egg dish. No specific cheesy ham omelette code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for cheesy ham omelette identified in available clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cheesy ham omelette serving is approximately 200 g, comprising two to three eggs folded with sliced ham and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, cooked, scrambled / omelette; Ham, sliced, regular; Cheese, cheddar — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a cheesy ham omelette as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: whole eggs cooked (FDC 172185), cured ham sliced (FDC 174608), and cheddar cheese (FDC 173414), combined in a representative ratio of approximately 55% egg, 25% ham, 20% cheddar by weight, consistent with a standard two-egg omelette with 50 g ham and 40 g cheese per 200 g serving. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a cheesy ham omelette. Eggs have a GI of approximately 0 (negligible carbohydrate); ham and cheddar cheese are similarly very low in digestible carbohydrate. GI estimated at 15 by analogy with egg-based dishes and high-protein, high-fat low-carbohydrate foods documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 1.2 × 200/100) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured ham and cheese
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 1040 mg sodium, representing over 45% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium ham and cheese alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 15, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is generally well-tolerated by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of insulin resistance management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from cheese and cured ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 5.8 g saturated fat and 310 mg cholesterol per 100 g, alongside high sodium from processed ham, makes this dish a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with established CVD should limit frequency and portion size and consider lower-fat cheese and unprocessed lean protein alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Cheesy Ham Omelette is a pan-fried egg dish made by whisking two to three whole eggs and cooking them in butter over medium heat, then folding in slices of cured ham and melted cheese — typically cheddar or processed cheese — to form a soft, golden omelette. The dish is popular in Malaysian kopitiam (coffee shop) settings and Western-style café menus across Singapore and Malaysia, often served as a breakfast or brunch item alongside toast and coffee. The texture is tender and slightly custardy on the inside with a lightly browned exterior; the flavour is rich, savoury, and mildly salty from the ham and cheese. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g) and fat (14.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The dish is a significant source of dietary cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium due to the cured ham and cheese components, which are relevant considerations for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides useful micronutrients including vitamin B12, vitamin A, riboflavin, phosphorus, and calcium from the egg and dairy components.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a ham and cheese omelette?
Is ham omelette good for diabetics?
Can I eat ham omelette if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a cheesy ham omelette as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for eggs, ham, and cheddar cheese in representative proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on egg-based and high-protein low-carbohydrate food data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality and the proportions are consistent with a standard preparation.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (eggs FDC 172185, ham FDC 174608, cheddar FDC 173414) in a ratio of approximately 55% egg, 25% ham, 20% cheddar by weight. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — eggs, ham, and cheddar cheese contain no dietary fibre). GI estimated at 15 by analogy with egg-based low-carbohydrate dishes per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA component data and are not independently verified for this composite dish. is_halal is set to false because standard cured ham contains pork; a halal version would require halal-certified turkey or beef ham. The food is listed under Malaysian cuisine per the input record and is served at EC COFFEE BAR on Carpenter Street, Singapore, at SGD 19.30.
